topic: A person learns their apartment building is going condo
raw_output:
[story 1]

The mail slot felt cold against my fingertips as I pulled out the official notice. The glossy paper, stark white against the worn wood of the hallway, seemed to mock the warmth I’d tried to cultivate in this space. It detailed the impending conversion of the building into luxury condominiums. I stood there for a long time, the silence of the hallway amplifying the frantic drumming in my chest. Every floorboard creak sounded like a final countdown. I slowly crumpled the paper, the sound a small, defeated sigh escaping my lips. I walked back to my apartment, the familiar scent of old coffee suddenly tasting like ash. I stared at the peeling paint on the wall, tracing patterns that had once felt like home, now just markers of obsolescence.

[story 2]

"They're selling it off," the realtor had said, his voice smooth and utterly devoid of sympathy. I remember nodding, forcing a smile that felt brittle on my face. Now, sitting on the edge of my sofa, I just stared at the framed photograph on the mantelpiece. The light catching the glass made the image of my old living room look painfully bright, a memory I was suddenly terrified to touch. My hands were shaking slightly as I picked up a chipped ceramic mug, the warmth immediately leaching away. I kept running my thumb over the rim, a repetitive, meaningless gesture. The air in the room felt thick, suffocating, as if the very atmosphere was heavy with unspoken loss.

[story 3]

I kept glancing toward the lobby, half-expecting someone to emerge and deny the reality of the situation. But the lobby was empty, just the muted hum of the building’s ancient ventilation system. I paced the length of my small living area, my footsteps echoing too loudly on the bare floor. My jaw felt tight, clenched so hard my molars ached. I stopped by the window, looking down at the street, as if hoping the view would somehow rearrange itself into something kinder. It didn't. It just looked like pavement and indifferent traffic. I sank onto the floor, pulling my knees to my chest, and let out a slow, ragged breath that hitched in my throat. The quiet was deafening.
